summary: O'Neil enthusiastically promotes Core Econ, a free online economics textbook that her colleague Suresh Naidu helped create. She criticizes traditional Econ 101 courses for teaching arbitrary mathematical models without questioning assumptions or providing data, describing her own negative experience at UC Berkeley. The Core Econ project aims to modernize economics education by incorporating real-world examples and the last three decades of economic research. O'Neil highlights how the curriculum tackles relevant topics like Apple and Foxconn, making economics actually useful for understanding current events.
key_points: Traditional Econ 101 courses fail students by presenting mathematical models as fact without questioning assumptions or providing supporting data
The current economics curriculum acts as a filter that selects for students who are 'either religious or willing to say anything for money'
Graduate-level economics is more rigorous, but the introductory bullshit creates a problematic gateway
Core Econ modernizes the curriculum with real-world examples and contemporary economic research
Free, high-quality educational resources can compete with expensive standard textbooks
money_quotes: I couldn't figure out why anyone would think that people behaved according to arbitrary mathematical rules.
There was no discussion of whether the assumptions were valid, no data to back it up.
I decided that anybody who kept going had to be either religious or willing to say anything for money.
Econ 101 is a terrible gateway for the subject, letting in people who are mostly kind of weird.
The sky's the limit when you get through the bullshit at the beginning.
Teaching economics as if the last three decades happened.
Like, it might actually help you understand the newspaper!! Can you imagine that?
key_insight: Economics education desperately needs reform because traditional Econ 101 courses teach detached mathematical models as dogma, filtering out skeptical thinkers and failing to connect theory to real-world evidence.
